questions.q: How does Angular change detection work? Explain Zone.js, dirty checking, and OnPush.

answer.oneLiner: Zone.js watches async events and tells Angular to check the component tree. Angular then walks every component top-down and compares bindings. OnPush lets a component skip that check unless its input reference changes, an event fires inside it, or an async pipe emits.
answer.detail: Zone.js patches browser APIs like setTimeout, fetch, and addEventListener. When any of those complete, Zone.js notifies Angular to start a change detection cycle. Angular then walks the component tree from the root downward, checking every bound expression. With the default strategy this happens for every component. With OnPush, Angular skips a component unless one of four things happens: an @Input reference changes, a DOM event fires inside the component, an async pipe emits, or markForCheck() is called manually. Signals (v16+) go further - they bypass Zone.js entirely by notifying only the exact views that depend on a changed signal.
keyPoints: Zone.js patches async APIs and triggers a CD cycle on completion
Default strategy checks every component in the tree on every cycle
OnPush skips a component unless input reference, event, async emit, or markForCheck()
Signals enable zoneless fine-grained updates with no Zone.js scanning

code.note: OnPush with a signal input means Angular only runs CD for this component when the signal value changes.

answer.analogy: Default CD is checking every room in a hotel; OnPush only checks rooms where the door opened.
redFlags: Not knowing what Zone.js does or why it exists
Mutating an array in place and expecting OnPush to detect the change
Confusing markForCheck() with detectChanges() - one schedules, one runs immediately

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you mutate an @Input array on an OnPush component?
followUpsQA.a: The reference stays the same so OnPush skips the check and the view goes stale - you must spread a new array instead.

followUpsQA.q: When would you call markForCheck() instead of detectChanges()?
followUpsQA.a: markForCheck() flags the component path dirty for the next scheduled cycle; use it for WebSocket pushes into an OnPush component when you want Angular to schedule the update, not run it synchronously.

followUpsQA.q: How do signals remove the need for Zone.js?
followUpsQA.a: When a signal changes it pushes a dirty flag directly to the views that read it, so Angular can refresh only those views without Zone.js scanning the whole tree.

followUpsQA.q: Can you use OnPush without immutable data patterns?
followUpsQA.a: Technically yes if you call markForCheck() manually, but that defeats the purpose and makes updates harder to track.

dialogue.text: Walk me through how Angular change detection works - Zone.js, dirty checking, all of it.

Zone.js patches all async browser APIs - setTimeout, fetch, addEventListener. When any of those complete, Zone.js notifies Angular to start a cycle, and Angular then walks the component tree top-down checking every binding. That is the default strategy.
That sounds expensive for a large tree. What does OnPush buy you?

OnPush tells Angular to skip that component's subtree entirely unless an @Input reference changes, a DOM event fires inside it, an async pipe emits, or you call markForCheck(). For a product list with 200 rows this cuts most of the CD work.
What is the classic mistake people make with OnPush?
Mutating an array in place - doing this.items.push(x) keeps the same reference, so OnPush sees no change. You need this.items = [...this.items, x] to get a new reference.
How do signals fit into this picture?

Signals skip Zone.js entirely. When a signal changes it marks only its dependent views dirty, so Angular refreshes targeted nodes without scanning anything else. This is the basis for zoneless Angular.
What is the difference between markForCheck and detectChanges?

markForCheck schedules a check up the tree for the next CD cycle; detectChanges runs CD synchronously right now for that view. I use markForCheck for async pushes like WebSocket data into an OnPush component.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Angular route guards? Explain CanActivate, CanDeactivate, CanActivateChild, and Resolve.

answer.oneLiner: Route guards are functions that run before or after navigation to decide whether it should proceed. CanActivate blocks entry, CanDeactivate prevents leaving, CanActivateChild guards child routes, and Resolve pre-fetches data before the component mounts.
answer.detail: Modern Angular uses functional guards - plain functions that use inject() - instead of injectable classes. CanActivate can return a boolean, a Promise of boolean, an Observable of boolean, or a UrlTree to redirect. Returning false cancels navigation; returning a UrlTree redirects. CanDeactivate receives the component instance so you can check a dirty form. Resolve delays navigation until an observable completes, putting data in the activated route's data map. CanMatch (v14+) decides whether a route config should even match, which is useful for lazy loading.
keyPoints: CanActivate - return true, false, or a UrlTree to redirect rather than block
CanDeactivate - gets the component instance; use it to check for unsaved changes
Resolve - blocks navigation until data is ready; read it from ActivatedRoute.snapshot.data
Modern guards are plain functions using inject(), not injectable classes

code.note: The factory pattern creates a reusable parameterised guard without a class.

answer.analogy: Guards are the security desk - they check your badge before you enter and stop you leaving with company property.
redFlags: Returning false instead of a UrlTree when you want to redirect - that just blocks with no feedback
Not knowing functional guards are the modern approach since Angular 14
Using Resolve on every route - it delays navigation and harms perceived performance

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between CanActivate and CanMatch?
followUpsQA.a: CanActivate runs after the router matches a route; CanMatch decides whether a route config matches at all, which is useful for conditionally lazy loading different modules for different user roles.

followUpsQA.q: How do you make a guard async - for example, checking the server?
followUpsQA.a: Return an Observable or Promise of boolean or UrlTree; the router waits for it to complete before proceeding.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if two CanActivate guards are on one route?
followUpsQA.a: They run sequentially in registration order and all must return true; if any returns false or a UrlTree, navigation stops.

followUpsQA.q: What is the performance trade-off with Resolve?
followUpsQA.a: Resolve delays the page until data is ready, which looks slow; fetching in ngOnInit with a skeleton gives better perceived speed for most screens.

dialogue.text: Explain route guards - CanActivate, CanDeactivate, CanActivateChild, and Resolve.

CanActivate blocks entering a route - I return true, false, or a UrlTree to redirect. CanDeactivate prevents leaving, and it gets the component instance so I can check a dirty form. CanActivateChild does the same but for child routes. Resolve pre-fetches data before the component mounts.
How do you redirect in a guard instead of just blocking?
Return a UrlTree created with router.createUrlTree(['/access-denied']). Returning false just cancels navigation with no redirect, which is confusing for the user.
What is the modern syntax for writing guards?
Functional guards - just a function that uses inject(). For parameterised ones like role checks I use a factory that returns a CanActivateFn. No class needed.
When would you avoid using Resolve?
On most screens I skip it and fetch in ngOnInit with a skeleton state. Resolve makes navigation visually freeze while the data loads, which feels slower even if the total time is the same.
What is CanMatch and how does it differ from CanActivate?

CanMatch runs before the router commits to a route config, so if it returns false the router keeps looking at other routes. This lets you serve different lazy-loaded modules to different user roles - the unauthorised chunk is never downloaded.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap?

answer.oneLiner: All four map each outer emission to an inner Observable and flatten the result, but they handle concurrent inner Observables differently. switchMap cancels the previous, concatMap queues them in order, mergeMap runs them in parallel, and exhaustMap ignores new outer values while one is still in flight.
answer.detail: Picking the wrong operator causes real bugs in production. Using mergeMap for a search box means results can arrive out of order and you show stale data. Using switchMap for a file upload queue cancels previous uploads when new files are added. Using mergeMap for a payment button means double-clicking fires two payment requests. Each operator has one natural home: switchMap for type-ahead, exhaustMap for form submits, concatMap for ordered write queues, mergeMap when order and concurrency do not matter.
keyPoints: switchMap - cancels the previous inner observable; ideal for type-ahead search
concatMap - queues new values and processes in order; ideal for sequential saves
mergeMap - runs all inner observables concurrently; use when order does not matter
exhaustMap - ignores new outer values while busy; ideal for login or payment buttons

code.note: switchMap unsubscribes the previous HTTP call when the user types again, preventing stale results from overwriting newer ones.

answer.analogy: switchMap is a barista who abandons your old order the moment you change your mind.
redFlags: Using mergeMap for a type-ahead search - results arrive out of order and overwrite each other
Using switchMap for a payment or upload queue - it cancels in-progress transactions
Not knowing these operators at a senior level - this is a very common interview filter question

followUpsQA.q: What actually happens to the HTTP call when switchMap switches?
followUpsQA.a: switchMap unsubscribes from the previous inner observable, which causes Angular's HttpClient to cancel the underlying HTTP request - no wasted network round-trip.

followUpsQA.q: Why does exhaustMap suit a login button?
followUpsQA.a: While the login request is in flight, exhaustMap drops all new click events, preventing double submissions even if the user clicks repeatedly.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use concatMap over mergeMap for an upload queue?
followUpsQA.a: When the server processes files sequentially and order matters - concatMap waits for each upload to complete before starting the next, so the server receives them in the correct order.

followUpsQA.q: Can you combine operators - for example, switchMap with retry?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - put retry or catchError inside the switchMap callback so errors are handled per inner observable without killing the outer search stream.

dialogue.text: Explain the difference between switchMap, mergeMap, concatMap, and exhaustMap.

All four map each outer emission to an inner observable and flatten it, but concurrency is different. switchMap cancels the previous inner one. concatMap queues them in order. mergeMap runs all in parallel. exhaustMap ignores new outer emissions while one inner is still running.
Give me a concrete example of choosing the wrong one causing a real bug.

Using mergeMap for a product search box. The user types 'lap', then 'lapt'. Two requests go out. If the 'lap' response arrives after 'lapt', you show stale results. switchMap would have cancelled 'lap' the moment 'lapt' was typed.
What about for an e-commerce checkout payment button?
exhaustMap. While the payment request is in flight, any additional button clicks are dropped completely. switchMap would cancel the first payment when the user clicks again, which is far worse.
And for a document save queue where order matters?
concatMap. Each save waits for the previous to complete before starting. mergeMap would send them all at once and the server might process them out of order.
How do you handle errors inside switchMap without killing the outer stream?
Put catchError inside the switchMap callback so it handles errors per inner observable and returns an empty or fallback observable. The outer stream stays alive for the next search query.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Angular Signals and how do they work internally?

answer.oneLiner: Signals are synchronous reactive values that automatically track their dependencies. When you read a signal inside a computed() or effect(), Angular registers that as a dependency. When the signal changes, it marks only those dependents as dirty and lazily recomputes them on next read.
answer.detail: The model is push-pull: when a signal's value changes, it pushes a dirty flag down to every computed and view that read it. But it does not immediately recompute - those dependents pull the new value lazily when they are next read or when Angular needs to render. computed() is memoised - it caches its last result and only recomputes after a dependency actually changed. effect() is eager - it runs once immediately, tracks all signals read during that run, then re-runs whenever any of them change. Signals do not use Zone.js, which is why they enable zoneless Angular. v18 adds linkedSignal (a writable computed that can be locally overridden) and the resource() API for async data fetching.
keyPoints: Reading a signal inside a reactive context registers it as a dependency automatically
computed() is lazy and memoised - recomputes on next read after a dependency changes
effect() is eager - runs immediately and re-runs when any tracked signal changes
Push-pull model: changes push a dirty flag, consumers pull the new value lazily

code.note: computed() is for derived state; effect() is only for side effects like logging or DOM manipulation.

answer.analogy: A signal is a whiteboard number - anyone watching it updates automatically when you change it.
redFlags: Using effect() to derive and store state instead of computed() - this creates update cycles
Thinking signals are just a BehaviorSubject wrapper - they are a synchronous dependency-tracking primitive with no RxJS involved
Not knowing computed() is lazy and memoised - it does not recompute unless read after a dependency changes

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between signal.set() and signal.update()?
followUpsQA.a: set() replaces the value unconditionally; update() receives the current value and derives the next one, for example count.update(n => n + 1).

followUpsQA.q: Can you use signals in services outside of components?
followUpsQA.a: Yes, signals work anywhere; effect() needs an injection context or a manual EffectRef cleanup, but computed() and signal() are plain functions with no context requirement.

followUpsQA.q: What is linkedSignal added in v18?
followUpsQA.a: A writable signal that derives its initial value from a source signal but can be locally overridden - useful for form fields that should reset when the source changes but also accept user edits.

followUpsQA.q: How do toSignal() and toObservable() bridge signals and RxJS?
followUpsQA.a: toSignal() subscribes to an observable and exposes its latest value as a signal; toObservable() wraps a signal in an observable that emits on every change.

dialogue.text: Explain how Angular signals work internally.

When you read a signal inside a computed() or effect(), Angular registers it as a dependency in a context-local tracking list. When the signal's value changes, it pushes a dirty flag to all registered dependents. But it does not immediately recompute them - that happens lazily on next read.
So what is the difference between computed and effect in terms of timing?

computed() is lazy and memoised - it only recomputes when you actually read it after a dependency changed. effect() is eager - it runs immediately on creation and re-runs synchronously when any tracked signal changes.
What happens if someone uses effect() to write to another signal?

Angular will throw in development if you write to a signal from an effect without explicitly opting in with allowSignalWrites. The correct pattern is computed() for derived state; effect() is for side effects like analytics logging or imperative DOM work.
How do signals enable zoneless Angular?

Signals bypass Zone.js entirely. When a signal changes, it marks only the specific template views that read it as dirty. Angular can then refresh just those nodes without Zone.js scanning the whole component tree.
How do you bridge signals with an existing RxJS-based service?

toSignal() subscribes to an observable and exposes its latest value as a read-only signal, handling unsubscription automatically. toObservable() does the reverse - emits on every signal change as an observable.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is the trackBy function in ngFor/@for and why is it important for performance?

answer.oneLiner: trackBy gives each list item a stable unique identity. When the list changes, Angular reuses existing DOM nodes for items whose identity matches instead of destroying and recreating the whole list.
answer.detail: Without trackBy, Angular compares items by object reference. When a list reloads from an API, every item is a new JavaScript object even if the data is identical. Angular sees all new references and destroys and recreates every DOM node. With trackBy returning a stable id like item.id, Angular only creates nodes for genuinely new items and updates only the properties of unchanged nodes. This is critical for large lists and preserves child component state like focus position or expanded accordion state. Angular 17's @for makes track mandatory, eliminating the common mistake of forgetting it.
keyPoints: Without track, a new array reference causes every DOM node to be destroyed and recreated
trackBy should return a stable unique identifier - usually item.id from your database
Preserving DOM nodes keeps child component state like focus, animations, and local state
Angular 17 @for makes track mandatory at compile time - you cannot forget it

code.note: Tracking by order.id means a status change only updates that one row's DOM - no full re-render.

answer.analogy: trackBy is like name badges at a conference - you update someone's role sticker, not replace the whole person.
redFlags: Not knowing what trackBy does - this is a basic performance question for any Angular developer
Tracking by index when items can be reordered or inserted - this defeats the purpose
Not knowing Angular 17 @for requires track - it is no longer optional

followUpsQA.q: What is the impact on child components when DOM nodes are destroyed and recreated?
followUpsQA.a: Every child component is destroyed and re-initialised, losing all local state including form input values, expanded state, scroll position, and running animations.

followUpsQA.q: When is it acceptable to track by index?
followUpsQA.a: Only for static display lists where items never reorder, never get inserted in the middle, and no child component holds local state - for example, a read-only table of fixed configuration values.

followUpsQA.q: How does Angular 17 @for enforce track at compile time?
followUpsQA.a: The compiler makes the track expression a required part of the @for syntax and throws a template error if it is missing, unlike *ngFor where trackBy was silently optional.

followUpsQA.q: Can tracking by a non-unique value cause issues?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - if two items share the same track value, Angular cannot distinguish them and may reuse the wrong DOM node, causing visual glitches or corrupted state.

dialogue.text: What does trackBy do in ngFor, and why does it matter for performance?

Without trackBy, Angular compares list items by object reference. When a list reloads from an API every item is a new object, so Angular destroys and recreates every DOM node even if the data is identical. trackBy gives each item a stable identity like its database id, so Angular only touches nodes that actually changed.
What are the side effects of destroying and recreating those DOM nodes?

Every child component is destroyed and re-initialised. You lose local state - expanded accordions collapse, focused inputs lose focus, animations reset. On a bank transaction list with expandable rows this is very noticeable.
When would tracking by index be acceptable?

Only for static read-only lists that never reorder and have no child components with local state. The moment you allow sorting or insertion, index tracking breaks because Angular maps the wrong node to the wrong data.
What changed in Angular 17 with the new @for syntax?
track is now required syntax in @for - the template compiler throws if you omit it. With *ngFor, trackBy was just a function reference you could silently forget.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are pure and impure pipes in Angular?

answer.oneLiner: Pure pipes (the default) only re-run their transform() when the input value or arguments change by reference, so they are memoised and fast. Impure pipes run on every change detection cycle regardless of input change, which is expensive but necessary for pipes that react to mutable state over time.
answer.detail: Angular marks a pipe pure by default (pure: true). When transform() has the same input references as last time, Angular skips it and returns the cached result. This is why you cannot filter a mutable array with a pure pipe - pushing to the array does not change the reference, so the pipe never re-runs. The correct fix is to return a new array reference and keep the pipe pure. Impure pipes (pure: false) run every CD cycle, which is why a list page with ten impure pipes can get noticeably slow. The async pipe is deliberately impure because it must react to observable emissions over time, not just reference changes.
keyPoints: Pure pipes are memoised - transform() only called when input reference or args change
Impure pipes run on every CD cycle - expensive, use only when necessary
The async pipe is impure by design so it can react to observable emissions
Fix mutable-array pipes by returning a new array reference, not by making the pipe impure

code.note: The currency pipe is safely pure because numbers are primitives - reference equals value change.

answer.analogy: A pure pipe is a cached calculator result; an impure pipe recalculates every second whether you need it or not.
redFlags: Making a filter or sort pipe impure because the mutable array does not trigger it - the real fix is immutable updates
Not knowing the async pipe is impure - this comes up when explaining why templates subscribe correctly
Adding multiple impure pipes to a large list template without realising the CD cost

followUpsQA.q: Why does a pure filter pipe not work with a mutable array?
followUpsQA.a: Pushing to an array does not change its reference, so Angular's pure pipe check sees the same reference and skips transform(). The solution is to spread a new array in the component before passing it to the template.

followUpsQA.q: Why is the async pipe impure?
followUpsQA.a: It must react to each observable emission over time, not just a one-time reference change, so it runs on every CD cycle to check whether a new value has arrived.

followUpsQA.q: What is the performance cost of multiple impure pipes on a large list?
followUpsQA.a: Each pipe's transform() runs on every CD cycle for every row, so a list of 100 rows with three impure pipes means 300 function calls per cycle, which can dominate frame time.

followUpsQA.q: Can you cache results manually inside an impure pipe?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - you can hold state in the pipe class and return the cached result when inputs have not changed, effectively making it semi-pure, though a pure pipe with immutable inputs is cleaner.

dialogue.text: What is the difference between a pure and an impure pipe?

Pure pipes are memoised - Angular only calls transform() when the input reference or arguments change. If they are the same as last time, it returns the cached result. Impure pipes run on every CD cycle regardless, which is expensive.
Give me an example of when a pipe must be impure.
The async pipe is the canonical example. It needs to react to each observable emission over time, not just when its reference changes, so it has to be impure.
A developer makes a filter pipe to filter a list of insurance claims by status. It does not work. Why?

They are probably mutating the array in place with push or splice. A pure pipe sees the same array reference and skips transform(). The fix is to do the filter in the component and emit a new array reference - not to make the pipe impure.
What is the cost of having ten impure pipes on a claims list with 500 rows?
Ten transform() calls per row, per CD cycle - 5000 calls every time anything triggers change detection. That will visibly hurt frame rate on a busy screen.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Angular HTTP Interceptors and what are common use cases?

answer.oneLiner: HTTP interceptors are middleware functions that sit on every HttpClient request and response pipeline. They let you add auth headers, handle errors, show loading indicators, and log requests centrally without touching individual service calls.
answer.detail: Modern Angular uses functional interceptors - plain HttpInterceptorFn functions registered via withInterceptors([]). Requests are immutable so you must call req.clone({...}) to modify them. Interceptors run in registration order for outgoing requests and in reverse for responses. A common pattern is catching a 401 response, refreshing the token once using a BehaviorSubject to hold queued requests, then replaying them. For request-specific opt-outs, use an HttpContext token that the interceptor can read.
keyPoints: Requests are immutable - always clone with req.clone() before modifying
Modern syntax is HttpInterceptorFn registered via withInterceptors([]) in provideHttpClient()
Registration order matters - requests flow in order, responses unwind in reverse
Use HttpContext tokens to pass metadata flags that interceptors can check

code.note: The interceptor clones the immutable request and adds the Bearer token once for every HTTP call in the app.

answer.analogy: An interceptor is the security gate every employee badge swipes through - the gate handles auth, not each desk.
redFlags: Mutating req directly instead of cloning - HttpRequest is immutable and direct mutation is silently ignored
Not knowing the modern functional interceptor syntax - class-based interceptors are legacy
Not knowing interceptor order - debugging a reversed response pipeline is painful if you do not know this

followUpsQA.q: How do you prevent a specific request from going through an interceptor?
followUpsQA.a: Create an HttpContext token, pass it in the request options, and check it inside the interceptor - for example, to skip the auth header on a public file upload endpoint.

followUpsQA.q: How do you implement a token refresh interceptor that queues concurrent requests?
followUpsQA.a: Catch the 401 error, check a BehaviorSubject flag - if already refreshing, queue with switchMap on the subject; if not, trigger a refresh, update the subject, then retry queued requests with the new token.

followUpsQA.q: Why must you clone the request?
followUpsQA.a: HttpRequest objects are immutable by design - Angular prevents accidental mutation of shared request objects that might be reused, so all modifications must go through req.clone().

followUpsQA.q: Can interceptors return an observable without calling next?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - you can short-circuit the request and return a cached observable directly from the interceptor, which is how response caching interceptors work.

dialogue.text: What are HTTP interceptors and what are the common use cases?

Interceptors sit in the HttpClient pipeline on every request and response. The classic uses are adding auth tokens to outgoing requests, catching 401 errors to refresh tokens, showing a global loading spinner, and logging or monitoring all calls centrally.
How do you add an auth header in an interceptor?
Requests are immutable, so I call req.clone({ headers: req.headers.set('Authorization', 'Bearer ' + token) }) and pass the cloned request to next(). Never mutate req directly.
What is the order guarantee when you have multiple interceptors?

They run in registration order for outgoing requests - so logging runs first, then auth. Responses unwind in reverse - auth unwraps first, then logging. The order matters for things like logging before and after auth headers are added.
How do you handle a 401 that requires a token refresh without cancelling parallel requests?

I use a BehaviorSubject as a refresh lock. The first 401 triggers a token refresh and sets the lock. Other 401s queue by doing switchMap on that subject. When the refresh completes the lock emits the new token and all queued requests retry with it.
How do you skip an interceptor for a specific public endpoint?
I create an HttpContext token like SKIP_AUTH and pass it in the request options. The interceptor checks context.get(SKIP_AUTH) and calls next(req) unchanged if it is set.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Deferrable Views (@defer) in Angular 17?

answer.oneLiner: @defer is template-level syntax that lazily loads a block of UI on a trigger such as the block entering the viewport, idle browser time, or user interaction. The compiler code-splits the block into a separate chunk automatically, cutting the initial bundle.
answer.detail: @defer works at the template level, unlike lazy routes which apply to entire routed components. The triggers include on viewport (Intersection Observer), on idle (requestIdleCallback), on interaction (click or focus), on hover, on timer(N), and a when condition. The @placeholder block shows before loading starts. The @loading block shows during the network fetch. The @error block shows on failure. You can combine a rendering trigger with a prefetch trigger - for example, prefetch on idle so the chunk downloads in the background while the user is reading, then render on viewport when they scroll down. The compiler automatically moves the deferred component out of the main bundle.
keyPoints: Triggers: on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on hover, on timer, when (boolean condition)
@placeholder shows before loading, @loading during fetch, @error on failure
prefetch trigger loads the chunk in the background on a separate trigger
Works at template level on any block - not just whole routes

code.note: The analytics dashboard chunk is prefetched during browser idle time and rendered when scrolled into view.

answer.analogy: Deferrable views are like a waiter who prepares your dessert while you eat your main course, not before you even sit down.
redFlags: Not knowing @defer as a senior developer - it is a headline Angular 17 feature
Thinking lazy loading only works at the route level - @defer works on any template block
Confusing @placeholder with @loading - placeholder shows before any loading begins, loading shows during the fetch

followUpsQA.q: How does @defer differ from lazy route loading?
followUpsQA.a: Lazy routes defer entire routed components at navigation boundaries. @defer defers any template block on any trigger, so you can lazy-load a comment section, a heavy chart, or a modal body within a single page.

followUpsQA.q: What does the minimum option on @loading do?
followUpsQA.a: It ensures the loading state shows for at least that duration so it does not flash briefly for fast connections, which looks like a visual glitch.

followUpsQA.q: Can you combine multiple triggers?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - you can separate triggers with a semicolon, for example on interaction and on timer(5000) so the block loads on click or after 5 seconds, whichever comes first.

followUpsQA.q: Does @defer work with server-side rendering?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - when SSR is enabled, Angular can render the deferred content on the server for SEO and hydrate it on the client, or skip it server-side and defer genuinely to the client depending on the trigger.

dialogue.text: What are deferrable views with @defer in Angular 17?

@defer is template syntax that lazily loads a block of UI on a trigger. The compiler automatically code-splits the block into a separate chunk, so it does not bloat the initial bundle. Common triggers are on viewport, on idle, on interaction, and when a boolean condition is true.
How is this different from lazy route loading?

Lazy routes defer entire components at navigation boundaries. @defer works on any block inside any template - a comment section, a heavy chart widget, or a below-the-fold recommendations panel. No route change needed.
What are the @placeholder, @loading, and @error blocks for?
@placeholder shows before any loading starts - a skeleton or button. @loading shows while the chunk is being fetched. @error shows if the fetch fails. They give you full UX control at each stage.
What is the prefetch trigger and when would you use it?

prefetch loads the chunk in the background on one trigger while the content renders on another. For a healthcare dashboard I would do prefetch on idle so the chunk downloads while the user reads the summary, then render on viewport when they scroll to the detailed charts.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Route Resolvers in Angular and when should you use them?

answer.oneLiner: A route resolver pre-fetches data before the target route activates, so the component receives its data immediately on mount with no internal loading state. Navigation waits until the resolver observable completes.
answer.detail: Modern resolvers use the functional ResolveFn<T> type with inject(). The resolved data is available in ActivatedRoute.snapshot.data['key'] or through the route.data observable. The trade-off is that the URL does not change and the page does not appear until the data is ready, which makes slow responses feel like the app has frozen. For most pages this means a skeleton screen rendered in the component provides better perceived UX. Resolvers are best reserved for data that is truly essential to render - like an edit form that must have the existing record. If the resolver returns EMPTY or throws, navigation is cancelled. Always handle errors inside the resolver and redirect to a not-found or error page.
keyPoints: Data is available in the component immediately via ActivatedRoute.snapshot.data['key']
Navigation is blocked until the resolver observable completes
Return EMPTY to cancel navigation on error and redirect to a fallback route
Use functional ResolveFn<T> with inject() - class-based resolvers are legacy

code.note: Returning EMPTY after redirecting cancels navigation cleanly without leaving the router in a broken state.

answer.analogy: A resolver is a waiter who brings your bread before you sit down - useful, but if the kitchen is slow, you are standing in the doorway.
redFlags: Not handling resolver errors - an unhandled error can leave navigation permanently frozen
Using resolvers for optional data like sidebar widgets - it blocks the whole page for non-critical content
Not knowing that returning EMPTY cancels navigation, while a thrown error without a catchError may freeze the router

followUpsQA.q: What is the user experience difference between a resolver and fetching in ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: A resolver keeps the user on the current page with no visible transition until data arrives. Fetching in ngOnInit shows the new route immediately with a skeleton, which feels faster even if the data takes the same time to load.

followUpsQA.q: How does a child route access resolver data from its parent?
followUpsQA.a: The child's ActivatedRoute has a parent property, so route.parent!.snapshot.data['key'] walks up the hierarchy to read parent resolver data.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you forget to handle an error in a resolver?
followUpsQA.a: If the observable errors and there is no catchError, Angular's router catches it and navigation is cancelled, but the router may emit a NavigationError event and leave the app on the old URL with no user feedback.

followUpsQA.q: Can multiple resolvers run in parallel on one route?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - Angular runs all resolvers for a route in parallel and waits for all of them to complete before activating the route.

dialogue.text: What is a route resolver and when do you use one?

A resolver pre-fetches data before the route activates, so the component has its data ready immediately on mount with no loading state inside it. Navigation waits until the resolver observable completes.
How does the component actually access that data?
Through ActivatedRoute - either ActivatedRoute.snapshot.data['policyData'] for a one-time read, or the route.data observable if the resolver can re-run on param changes.
What is the performance trade-off versus fetching in ngOnInit?

With a resolver, navigation is blocked and the URL does not change until data arrives - a slow API makes it feel like the app froze. ngOnInit with a skeleton renders immediately and shows a loading state, which feels faster even if total time is the same. I only use resolvers for data that is truly essential to render at all.
What happens if the resolver fails - for example, a 404?

I catch the error inside the resolver with catchError, redirect to a not-found page, and return EMPTY. Returning EMPTY cancels navigation cleanly. If I let the error propagate uncaught, the router emits a NavigationError and the user gets no feedback.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How does Angular's ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush optimization work in practice?

answer.oneLiner: OnPush tells Angular to skip a component's change detection unless one of four things happens: an @Input reference changes, a DOM event fires inside the component, an async pipe emits a new value, or markForCheck() is called. This avoids scanning the whole subtree on every cycle.
answer.detail: OnPush requires treating all data as immutable. If you push to an array or mutate an object, the reference stays the same and OnPush never detects the change. You must spread new values: this.orders = [...this.orders, newOrder]. When data comes from outside the event flow - like a WebSocket push - Angular's Zone.js does not know about it, so you must call markForCheck() to schedule a check. Signal inputs work even better with OnPush because they propagate changes without needing markForCheck().
keyPoints: OnPush re-checks on: @Input reference change, DOM event, async pipe emission, markForCheck()
Immutability is non-negotiable - mutation without reference change means the view goes stale
markForCheck() schedules a dirty-flag up the tree for the next cycle; detectChanges() runs synchronously now
Signal inputs pair naturally with OnPush and eliminate the need for manual markForCheck()

code.note: A signal input automatically propagates its change through OnPush without any manual ChangeDetectorRef call.

answer.analogy: OnPush is a hotel housekeeper who only enters a room when the Do Not Disturb sign is removed.
redFlags: Mutating arrays or objects and expecting OnPush to detect the change - this is the most common OnPush bug in production
Not knowing the difference between markForCheck and detectChanges - using detectChanges in a service can cause ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError
Not realising the async pipe automatically calls markForCheck on each emission

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between markForCheck() and detectChanges()?
followUpsQA.a: markForCheck() walks up the tree and marks the path dirty so the next scheduled CD cycle includes this component. detectChanges() runs CD synchronously right now for this view and its children, regardless of the schedule.

followUpsQA.q: How does the async pipe work with OnPush?
followUpsQA.a: The async pipe subscribes to the observable and calls markForCheck() on every emission, which is why using it in an OnPush component template keeps the view up to date without any manual intervention.

followUpsQA.q: What is the risk of using detach() on a component?
followUpsQA.a: The component is completely removed from change detection until you reattach or call detectChanges() manually. Any data updates are invisible to the view, which can cause very hard-to-debug staleness bugs.

followUpsQA.q: How do signal inputs improve OnPush components?
followUpsQA.a: Signal inputs automatically notify Angular when their value changes, so Angular can mark only the reading views dirty without Zone.js and without manual markForCheck() calls.

dialogue.text: How does OnPush work in practice on a real application?

OnPush skips a component's check unless an @Input reference changes, a DOM event fires inside it, an async pipe emits, or I call markForCheck(). On a logistics dashboard with 50 shipment rows this cuts most of the CD work on every user interaction.
Show me an update pattern that works and one that silently breaks it.

this.shipments = [...this.shipments, newShipment] works because it creates a new array reference. this.shipments.push(newShipment) breaks it silently - same reference, OnPush skips the check, the new row never appears.
What about live tracking updates coming in from a WebSocket?

Zone.js does not patch WebSocket messages reliably, so I update the data and call this.cd.markForCheck(). Alternatively I expose the WebSocket stream through a BehaviorSubject and use the async pipe in the template, which calls markForCheck automatically.
What is the difference between markForCheck and detectChanges?

markForCheck schedules a dirty-flag up the component tree for the next CD cycle - async and safe. detectChanges runs CD synchronously right now, which can cause ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError if called at the wrong time in services.
How do signal inputs improve this further?
A signal input automatically tells Angular when its value changed. Angular marks only the views that read it as dirty, no Zone.js and no manual markForCheck() call needed.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are Angular Schematics and how does the CLI use them?

answer.oneLiner: Schematics are code-transformation scripts that operate on a virtual file system. The Angular CLI uses them to power ng generate, ng add, and ng update - scaffolding files, configuring libraries, and migrating code across major versions.
answer.detail: A schematic is a Rule function that receives a virtual Tree - a representation of the file system - and returns a modified Tree. All changes are collected in a staging area and only written to disk if the entire schematic succeeds, making operations atomic. Schematics can read and write files, add imports, modify decorator metadata, and run AST transforms using TypeScript's AST API. ng generate runs built-in CLI schematics. ng add installs a package and runs its schematic to configure it. ng update runs migration schematics that rewrite existing code for breaking API changes across Angular major versions, which is why you should always use ng update rather than a plain npm upgrade when upgrading Angular.
keyPoints: A schematic is a Rule function operating on a virtual Tree - atomic and rollback-safe
ng generate runs built-in schematics; ng add runs a library's configuration schematic
ng update runs migration schematics that automatically rewrite code for breaking changes
Custom schematics let teams enforce architecture patterns

code.note: ng update does more than a version bump - it runs automated code migrations that would otherwise take hours of manual work.

answer.analogy: A schematic is a factory stamp - run it and every generated file follows the exact same pattern.
redFlags: Upgrading Angular with npm install directly and skipping ng update - you miss all the automated migrations
Not knowing what runs behind ng generate and ng add - this is fundamental CLI knowledge for senior developers
Not knowing schematics are atomic - this is important when explaining why partial runs are safe

followUpsQA.q: How would you write a custom schematic to enforce team conventions?
followUpsQA.a: Author a Rule that uses the Tree API to create files from templates, add imports to module or route files using AST transforms, and publish it as an npm schematic collection that your team runs with ng generate.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between ng add and npm install?
followUpsQA.a: npm install just adds the package; ng add installs it and runs the package's schematic to configure it - for example, ng add @angular/material adds the package, imports BrowserAnimationsModule, sets up a theme, and updates angular.json.

followUpsQA.q: What does atomicity mean for schematics?
followUpsQA.a: All file changes are collected in the virtual Tree and only written to disk if the entire schematic completes without error - no partially generated files if something goes wrong midway.

followUpsQA.q: How do migration schematics know what to rewrite?
followUpsQA.a: They use the TypeScript compiler API to parse the AST of your source files, identify deprecated usages by import path and symbol name, and replace them with the new API.

dialogue.text: What are Angular schematics?

Schematics are code-transformation scripts that operate on a virtual file Tree. The CLI uses them internally for ng generate, ng add, and ng update. They scaffold files, configure libraries, and migrate breaking API changes automatically.
How do they actually work - what is the virtual Tree?

The Tree is an in-memory representation of the file system. A schematic is a Rule function that reads and writes to the Tree. All changes are staged and only written to disk atomically when the whole schematic succeeds - no partial scaffolding if something fails.
Why use ng update instead of just bumping the version in package.json?

ng update runs migration schematics that use the TypeScript AST to rewrite your source code for breaking changes. For Angular 18 that includes converting ViewChild to signal-based viewChild and updating deprecated providers. A version bump in package.json just changes a number - your code still has the old API.
How would you use a custom schematic for your team?

I would write a schematic that generates a feature folder with a routing file, a shell component, a service, and a barrel index.ts all pre-wired together. Developers run ng generate my-team:feature cart instead of creating each file manually.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How would you optimise a slow Angular application? What tools and techniques would you use?

answer.oneLiner: Profile first to find the real bottleneck - Angular DevTools for change detection overhead, a bundle analyser for size. Then apply the right fix: OnPush or signals for CD cost, lazy routes and @defer for bundle size, track in loops, pure pipes over template methods, and SSR for Core Web Vitals.
answer.detail: Common performance killers in Angular apps: method calls in templates that run on every CD cycle, large initial bundles from not using lazy loading, missing trackBy causing full list re-renders, impure pipes, default change detection on a deep component tree, and no OnPush with immutable data. The Angular DevTools profiler shows a flame chart of CD time per component. source-map-explorer or webpack-bundle-analyzer shows what is in the bundle. Fix in order of impact found by profiling, not by guessing. For Core Web Vitals (LCP, TBT) add SSR with ng add @angular/ssr and use @defer to cut the blocking JS.
keyPoints: Profile before optimising - Angular DevTools flame chart and bundle analyser show actual bottlenecks
OnPush + immutable data cuts most CD overhead; signals eliminate Zone.js scanning entirely
Lazy routes and @defer blocks cut initial bundle size and Time to Blocking
Never call methods in templates - use signals, computed values, or pure pipes instead

code.note: A pure pipe or computed signal eliminates thousands of redundant function calls per second on a busy table.

answer.analogy: Optimising without profiling is like prescribing medicine without a diagnosis - you might make it worse.
redFlags: Optimising before profiling - guessing wastes time and can introduce new bugs
Applying OnPush everywhere without switching to immutable data patterns - this just introduces stale view bugs
Not knowing Angular DevTools exists or how to read a CD profiler flame chart

followUpsQA.q: How do you find which component is causing excessive change detection?
followUpsQA.a: Open Angular DevTools in Chrome, go to the Profiler tab, start recording, interact with the app, then stop - the flame chart shows each component with its CD time and how often it ran.

followUpsQA.q: How do you identify what is bloating the bundle?
followUpsQA.a: Run ng build --stats-json and feed the output to webpack-bundle-analyzer, or use the Angular CLI's built-in source-map-explorer support to see which packages take up the most bytes.

followUpsQA.q: How does SSR improve Core Web Vitals?
followUpsQA.a: SSR sends rendered HTML to the browser so Largest Contentful Paint fires as soon as the HTML arrives, without waiting for JavaScript to download, parse, and execute.

followUpsQA.q: Why is calling a method in a template harmful?
followUpsQA.a: Angular cannot know if the method's return value has changed, so it calls it on every CD cycle. On a table with 200 rows this is 200 method calls per user interaction, even if nothing changed.

dialogue.text: Walk me through how you would optimise a slow Angular application.

First I profile - I would never guess. Angular DevTools gives a CD flame chart showing which components spend the most time per cycle. A bundle analyser shows what is inflating the initial download. Those two tools tell me where to focus.
Say the profiler shows the order table re-checks on every keystroke in a search box. What do you do?

Apply OnPush to the table component and make sure the parent passes a new array reference when the list changes. Or convert to signal inputs so Angular tracks only the rows affected. Also check for method calls in the template - replacing them with pure pipes or computed signals eliminates thousands of redundant calls.
What about a slow initial load - Time to Interactive is 8 seconds?

Bundle analyser first. Usually it is an unneeded large library, or a route that should be lazy-loaded but is included in the main chunk. I split routes with loadComponent, move heavy widgets to @defer blocks with on viewport triggers, and check that the SSR build is enabled for a fast first paint.
What one template pattern do you hunt for immediately?
Method calls in the template. Calling formatCurrency(item.amount) in a cell runs on every CD cycle for every row. I replace it with a pure pipe or a computed signal on the row component.
How does SSR fit into performance?

SSR sends rendered HTML so the browser paints content immediately - LCP fires before any JavaScript runs. Combined with @defer for below-the-fold content, it dramatically cuts both LCP and Total Blocking Time.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What is ViewEncapsulation (Emulated, None, ShadowDom), and how does viewProviders differ from it?

answer.oneLiner: ViewEncapsulation controls how a component's CSS is scoped to prevent leaking into other components. viewProviders is a completely separate concept - it scopes dependency injection providers to the component's own view, hiding them from projected content passed in via ng-content.
answer.detail: Emulated (the default) makes Angular add a unique attribute to every element in the component's template and prefix all CSS selectors with that attribute, so styles only match elements with that attribute. None removes all scoping - styles become global and leak everywhere. ShadowDom uses the browser's native Shadow DOM API, which provides true encapsulation but prevents global design system styles from styling inside the component. viewProviders is a DI concept: a service in providers is visible to the component's template and to any content projected via ng-content. A service in viewProviders is visible only to the component's own template children, not to projected slots.
keyPoints: Emulated (default) - Angular adds unique attributes and rewrites selectors; portable and works everywhere
None - styles become global; use only when you deliberately want global CSS
ShadowDom - true native isolation; blocks external design system styles from penetrating
viewProviders is DI scoping, not style scoping - hides providers from ng-content projected children

code.note: viewProviders hides BookingStateService from content projected into ng-content slots - the two concepts are completely independent.

answer.analogy: ViewEncapsulation is a paint booth that keeps your colour inside. viewProviders is a keycard that only lets your staff through the front door.
redFlags: Conflating viewProviders with style encapsulation - they solve entirely different problems and are often confused in interviews
Using ViewEncapsulation.None as the default and then debugging why styles are leaking everywhere
Reaching for ::ng-deep to style projected content instead of using CSS custom properties or restructuring the template

followUpsQA.q: What is the practical trade-off between Emulated and ShadowDom?
followUpsQA.a: Emulated works everywhere, allows global design system styles to reach into the component, and is the safe default. ShadowDom gives true isolation but blocks your Tailwind or Material Design variables from styling inside the component.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between providers and viewProviders?
followUpsQA.a: providers makes a service visible to the component's view and to any content projected via ng-content. viewProviders makes it visible only to the component's own view children, not to projected slots.

followUpsQA.q: Why was ::ng-deep deprecated?
followUpsQA.a: It pierces encapsulation boundaries and generates global CSS that is hard to track and can cause specificity conflicts. Angular recommends CSS custom properties for theming child component internals.

followUpsQA.q: When would you deliberately use ViewEncapsulation.None?
followUpsQA.a: For a global style override component that intentionally injects styles into the document, or for third-party widget wrappers where you need to style elements Angular does not own.

dialogue.text: Explain ViewEncapsulation and how viewProviders differs from it.

ViewEncapsulation is about CSS scoping - how to prevent a component's styles from leaking out. Emulated, the default, rewrites selectors with a unique attribute. None makes styles global. ShadowDom uses native browser encapsulation. viewProviders is a completely separate DI concept with nothing to do with styles.
What does viewProviders actually do?

It scopes a DI provider to the component's own view children only. If content is projected in via ng-content, those projected components cannot see services in viewProviders. Regular providers[] is visible to both own view and projected content.
When would you choose ShadowDom over Emulated?

For a widget library where I need true isolation so consumer applications cannot accidentally override internal styles. But I would not use it in a standard app because it blocks global design tokens and Tailwind utilities from reaching inside the component.
A developer uses ::ng-deep to style a projected child. What is wrong with that?

::ng-deep is deprecated. It generates global CSS, bypasses encapsulation, and causes specificity conflicts that are painful to debug. The right approach is CSS custom properties that the parent sets and the child reads.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you write a custom directive, and what makes a directive structural vs attribute?

answer.oneLiner: A directive is an @Directive class with a CSS selector. Attribute directives change the host element's appearance or behaviour using ElementRef and HostBinding. Structural directives add or remove DOM nodes by injecting TemplateRef and ViewContainerRef.
answer.detail: The distinction is what the directive does to the DOM. An attribute directive leaves the DOM structure intact and modifies the element it sits on. A structural directive controls whether nodes exist in the DOM at all - it stamps a template in or clears it. The asterisk prefix on *ngIf is syntactic sugar that wraps the host element in an ng-template, giving the directive access to TemplateRef. You can only have one structural directive per element because each needs its own ng-template wrapper. Use ng-container to apply multiple structural directives without adding an extra DOM node.
keyPoints: Attribute directive - manipulates the host element via ElementRef, Renderer2, or HostBinding
Structural directive - injects TemplateRef and ViewContainerRef to stamp or clear DOM nodes
The asterisk * is sugar that converts the element to an ng-template, giving the directive its TemplateRef
Only one structural directive per element - use ng-container to combine them

code.note: IfRoleDirective stamps or removes the host element based on the user's role - a real-world use case more useful than a plain *ngIf.

answer.analogy: An attribute directive is a sticker on a door; a structural directive decides whether the door exists at all.
redFlags: Not knowing that structural directives inject TemplateRef and ViewContainerRef - this is the defining difference
Confusing the asterisk sugar - not knowing it wraps the element in an ng-template
Using ElementRef.nativeElement directly in an attribute directive instead of HostBinding or Renderer2

followUpsQA.q: Why can you only have one structural directive per element?
followUpsQA.a: Each structural directive needs an ng-template wrapper. Putting two on one element would require two wrappers on the same element, which is ambiguous. Use ng-container to nest them instead.

followUpsQA.q: How does *appIfRole expand in the compiled template?
followUpsQA.a: The compiler desugars it to ng-template [appIfRole]="'ADMIN'" and moves the original element inside that ng-template as its content.

followUpsQA.q: Why use Renderer2 or HostBinding instead of ElementRef.nativeElement directly?
followUpsQA.a: Accessing nativeElement directly bypasses Angular's rendering pipeline and breaks with server-side rendering where there is no real DOM. Renderer2 abstracts the platform and HostBinding integrates with change detection.

followUpsQA.q: How do you pass context variables from a structural directive back to the template?
followUpsQA.a: Pass a context object as the second argument to createEmbeddedView() and access it in the template with let-variable using the directive's exported context properties.

dialogue.text: How do you write a custom directive and what makes it structural versus attribute?

Both use @Directive with a CSS selector. An attribute directive changes the element it sits on - colour, classes, event responses - using HostBinding and HostListener. A structural directive adds or removes DOM by injecting TemplateRef and ViewContainerRef and calling createEmbeddedView or clear.
What does the asterisk in *ngIf actually do?

It is syntactic sugar. The compiler wraps the host element in an ng-template and transforms *ngIf='cond' to ng-template [ngIf]='cond'. The directive then gets a TemplateRef to that template and stamps or clears it.
Give me a real use case for a custom structural directive.

A role guard directive - appIfRole='ADMIN' that injects AuthService, checks the user's roles, and either stamps the template or clears it. Cleaner than wrapping every admin UI element in *ngIf with a role check.
Why not just use ElementRef.nativeElement directly in a directive?

It bypasses Angular's rendering layer and breaks with SSR because there is no DOM on the server. HostBinding integrates with change detection; Renderer2 abstracts the platform so the same directive works in the browser and server.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding used for?

answer.oneLiner: @HostListener subscribes to DOM events on the host element and calls a handler method. @HostBinding binds a component or directive class property to a DOM property, attribute, class, or style of the host element. Both are used inside directives to affect the element they are applied to.
answer.detail: Together they let you build interactive attribute directives declaratively without manually querying the DOM or using Renderer2. HostBinding keeps state in sync with the host's DOM automatically through Angular's change detection. HostListener can capture not just element events but also global events like window:scroll or document:keydown by prefixing the event name. The modern alternative for components is using the host property in @Component({host: {}}) which is more AOT-friendly and slightly more performant.
keyPoints: @HostListener reacts to DOM events on the host - supports element events and global window/document events
@HostBinding reflects a class property onto a host DOM property, attribute, class, or style
Both avoid direct DOM manipulation and stay within Angular's rendering pipeline
The host:{} property in @Component is the modern alternative for components

code.note: HostBinding updates the class automatically through change detection; no manual DOM manipulation needed.

answer.analogy: HostListener is a motion sensor on the door; HostBinding is the light that turns on when the sensor fires.
redFlags: Manipulating the host element via ElementRef.nativeElement directly instead of HostBinding - breaks SSR and bypasses change detection
Not knowing @HostListener can listen to window and document events by prefixing with 'window:' or 'document:'
Not knowing these decorators work in components as well as directives - a component is a directive

followUpsQA.q: How do you pass the $event object to a HostListener handler?
followUpsQA.a: Add it as the second argument: @HostListener('keydown', ['$event']) onKey(event: KeyboardEvent). The string array maps positional arguments from the event to the handler parameters.

followUpsQA.q: How do you listen to window scroll with HostListener?
followUpsQA.a: @HostListener('window:scroll') onScroll() - the window: prefix tells Angular to attach the listener to the window object rather than the host element.

followUpsQA.q: What is the host property in @Component and how does it differ from decorators?
followUpsQA.a: The host property in @Component or @Directive metadata accepts an object of event and binding mappings: { '(click)': 'onClick()', '[class.active]': 'isActive' }. It is more AOT-friendly and collocates all host bindings in one place.

followUpsQA.q: Why does HostBinding work better than direct DOM writes for SSR?
followUpsQA.a: HostBinding runs through Angular's renderer abstraction layer, which on the server produces rendered HTML strings instead of DOM operations - so the same directive works in both environments.

dialogue.text: What are @HostListener and @HostBinding used for?

@HostListener subscribes to DOM events on the element the directive is applied to and calls a handler. @HostBinding binds a directive property to a host DOM property, class, style, or attribute. Together they let you build interactive directives declaratively without touching the DOM directly.
Give me a real directive example using both.

A drag-and-drop target directive: @HostBinding('class.drag-over') isDragOver = false; and two HostListeners for dragover and dragleave that flip that flag. The class appears and disappears automatically through Angular's CD - no querySelector, no class manipulation.
Why should you avoid ElementRef.nativeElement for this?

Direct DOM writes bypass Angular's renderer and break with server-side rendering because there is no DOM on the server. HostBinding goes through the renderer abstraction so it works in both environments.
How do you listen to a global window event from a directive?
Prefix with 'window:' - @HostListener('window:scroll') onScroll(). Angular attaches the listener to the window and removes it when the directive is destroyed, handling the cleanup for you.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you create a component manually, without the Angular CLI?

answer.oneLiner: Create a TypeScript class, add the @Component decorator with at least a selector and template, mark it standalone: true, then import it in any parent component's imports array and use its selector tag in a template.
answer.detail: The Angular CLI's ng generate command is just a convenience - all it does is create the .ts file, write the @Component decorator, and add imports. There is nothing the CLI does that you cannot do by hand. The minimum a component needs is a class with @Component providing a selector and either template or templateUrl. For a standalone component, you also add standalone: true (default in v17+) and list any Angular modules or other components it needs in imports: []. For non-standalone components, declare it in an NgModule's declarations array and export it if other modules need it.
keyPoints: Write a TypeScript class and decorate it with @Component with selector and template at minimum
For standalone: add standalone: true and list dependencies in imports: []
For NgModule-based: declare in the module's declarations and export if shared
The CLI generates the same code - it saves typing, not capability

code.note: A standalone component must be added to the parent's imports array or it renders as an unknown HTML element with no error.

answer.analogy: The CLI is autocomplete for component boilerplate - the @Component decorator is the real definition.
redFlags: Thinking the CLI is required to create a component - the decorator is what defines the component
Forgetting to add a standalone component to the parent's imports array - it silently renders as an empty custom HTML element
Not knowing the minimum required fields in @Component - just selector and template are enough

followUpsQA.q: What is the minimum a @Component decorator needs to be valid?
followUpsQA.a: A selector and either a template string or templateUrl. Styles and styleUrls are optional.

followUpsQA.q: What does the CLI generate beyond the .ts file?
followUpsQA.a: A spec file for unit tests, a separate HTML template file, a CSS file, and automatic import wiring in the parent module or route file depending on the flags used.

followUpsQA.q: How do you register a non-standalone component in an NgModule?
followUpsQA.a: Add it to the NgModule's declarations array. To make it available to other modules, also add it to exports.

followUpsQA.q: Why does Angular render nothing and show no error when a standalone component is missing from imports?
followUpsQA.a: Angular treats the unknown selector as a custom HTML element and silently renders an empty DOM node. strictTemplates in tsconfig would catch this as a compile error.

dialogue.text: How would you create a component without using the Angular CLI?

Create a TypeScript class and decorate it with @Component providing a selector and a template. For a standalone component I add standalone: true and list any dependencies like CommonModule or other components in imports: []. Then add it to the parent's imports array and use its selector tag.
What does the CLI actually add beyond what you just described?

A spec file, a separate HTML template file, a CSS file, and it wires the import into the parent file automatically. It is just code generation - the @Component decorator is all that matters to Angular.
A developer creates a standalone InvoiceBadgeComponent manually and uses its selector in a parent template, but nothing renders. What is wrong?

They forgot to add InvoiceBadgeComponent to the parent component's imports array. Angular does not know about standalone components unless they are explicitly imported. It silently treats the selector as an unknown custom HTML element.
Would you see an error in the console?

Not with the default settings. But with strictTemplates enabled in tsconfig, the Angular compiler would throw at build time saying the selector is not a known element. That is a very good reason to enable strict templates.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you scope styles to a particular component, and what does encapsulation guarantee?

answer.oneLiner: Put styles in the component's styles or styleUrls array. Angular's default Emulated encapsulation rewrites every CSS selector with a generated attribute unique to that component, so the styles only match elements in that component's own template.
answer.detail: Angular adds an attribute like _ngcontent-abc-c1 to every element the component creates. It then rewrites .card-title to .card-title[_ngcontent-abc-c1]. This means the same class name in two different components does not conflict. The :host selector targets the component's own host element. :host-context(parent-class) applies styles when an ancestor has a specific class, which is useful for dark mode or density themes. ::ng-deep pierces the encapsulation boundary and is deprecated - the correct alternative for theming child component internals is CSS custom properties.
keyPoints: Emulated adds a unique attribute to component elements and rewrites selectors - portable and the safe default
:host targets the component's own root element from inside its styles
:host-context() applies styles based on an ancestor class - useful for theme variations
Avoid ::ng-deep - it is deprecated and generates global CSS

code.note: CSS custom properties cross encapsulation boundaries by design, making them the safe replacement for ::ng-deep.

answer.analogy: Emulated encapsulation is like a name tag on each person - the same first name in two teams never collides.
redFlags: Using ::ng-deep as a default tool for styling children - it is deprecated and leaks global styles that are hard to debug
Not knowing how :host works - a common question for mid-level Angular developers
Assuming styles are globally scoped by default - they are scoped to the component with Emulated encapsulation

followUpsQA.q: How does Angular physically implement Emulated encapsulation?
followUpsQA.a: The Angular compiler adds a unique attribute (_ngcontent-xxx) to every element in the component's template and rewrites all CSS selectors to include that attribute as a combinator, so rules only match elements with that specific attribute.

followUpsQA.q: What is the use case for :host-context()?
followUpsQA.a: Applying different styles when an ancestor has a theme class - for example :host-context(.dark-theme) { background: #222 } applies dark styles to this component when any ancestor has .dark-theme.

followUpsQA.q: Why are CSS custom properties better than ::ng-deep for theming?
followUpsQA.a: Custom properties cross encapsulation boundaries by design without making styles global. The parent sets the property, the child reads it via var() - no selector leakage.

followUpsQA.q: Are component styles included in the global CSS bundle?
followUpsQA.a: With the modern application builder, component styles are either inlined with the component chunk or extracted per-route. They are not all dumped into a single global stylesheet.

dialogue.text: How do you scope styles to a component, and what does Angular's encapsulation actually guarantee?

Put styles in the styles or styleUrls array of @Component. With the default Emulated encapsulation, Angular adds a unique generated attribute to every element in the template and rewrites all CSS selectors to include that attribute. So .card-title in component A never affects .card-title in component B.
How do you style the component's own root element?

With :host. For example :host { display: block; padding: 16px; } targets the app-booking-card element itself. :host-context(.dark-theme) applies styles when an ancestor carries that class, which is how I handle dark mode without global CSS.
A developer uses ::ng-deep to reach into an Angular Material input inside their component. What is the problem?

::ng-deep is deprecated. It generates global CSS that leaks out of the component and can affect Material inputs everywhere in the app. If two components both use ::ng-deep on the same selector, they conflict and you have no way to isolate which wins.
What is the correct alternative?

CSS custom properties. The parent component defines a custom property like --input-text-color and the Material component's internal CSS reads it via var(). Custom properties cross encapsulation boundaries by design - no deprecated selectors, no global leakage.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: Why shouldn't the constructor do heavy work, and what belongs in ngOnChanges instead?

answer.oneLiner: The constructor runs before @Input values are set and before the view exists. It is only for dependency injection. Logic that reacts to input values belongs in ngOnChanges (fires on every @Input change with old and new values) or ngOnInit (fires once after the first inputs are set).
answer.detail: When Angular creates a component, the constructor runs first with no input values set - they are all undefined at that point. Angular then runs the first ngOnChanges pass, setting the initial input values and providing a SimpleChanges map of what changed and whether it is the first change. Then ngOnInit runs once. Heavy work in the constructor also hurts unit testing because it runs synchronously before you can set up mocks or input values. Async work like HTTP in the constructor creates timing issues and makes the component harder to test. With signal inputs, you can use computed() or effect() instead of ngOnChanges to react to input changes reactively.
keyPoints: Constructor: @Input values are all undefined - only inject dependencies here
ngOnChanges: fires on every @Input change with SimpleChanges providing old and new values
ngOnInit: fires once after the first inputs are bound - safe for initial data loading
Signal inputs react in computed() or effect() without needing ngOnChanges at all

code.note: ngOnChanges fires on the first input binding and on every subsequent change - use changes['policyId'].firstChange to distinguish.

answer.analogy: The constructor is the delivery address on a package - write it before the package is filled, not after.
redFlags: Reading @Input values in the constructor - they are undefined at that point with no exception thrown
Making HTTP calls in the constructor - hurts testability and creates a race condition with the view lifecycle
Not knowing what SimpleChanges provides - missing firstChange and previousValue checks is a common source of bugs

followUpsQA.q: What does the SimpleChanges object provide that a plain ngOnInit does not?
followUpsQA.a: It provides the currentValue, previousValue, and firstChange boolean for each changed input, letting you distinguish an initial binding from a subsequent change and compare old and new values.

followUpsQA.q: Why does HTTP in the constructor hurt testability?
followUpsQA.a: The HTTP call fires synchronously during construction before any test setup runs. You cannot inject a mock service or set input values before the call happens, making the component impossible to test in isolation.

followUpsQA.q: How do signal inputs change the need for ngOnChanges?
followUpsQA.a: Signal inputs are reactive values. You can use computed() to derive state from them or effect() to react to changes without ngOnChanges. Angular tracks reads automatically and updates only what depends on the changed signal.

followUpsQA.q: When does ngOnChanges fire relative to ngOnInit?
followUpsQA.a: ngOnChanges fires first, on the initial input binding and on every subsequent input change. ngOnInit fires once, after the first ngOnChanges. So ngOnChanges always runs at least once before ngOnInit.

dialogue.text: Why should the constructor not do heavy work, and what belongs in ngOnChanges?

The constructor runs before Angular sets any @Input values, so they are all undefined. Making an HTTP call there means calling the API with undefined parameters and makes the component untestable because the call fires before any mock can be injected.
So where should you react to an @Input change?

ngOnChanges for logic that depends on the input value itself - it fires on every input change and provides a SimpleChanges object with currentValue, previousValue, and a firstChange flag. ngOnInit for one-time setup after the first inputs are bound.
What does firstChange let you do that a plain ngOnInit check would not?

Distinguish between the initial binding and a subsequent parent update. For a policy details page I want to load the policy on first render but also reload it if the parent changes the policyId without destroying the component. ngOnInit only fires once; ngOnChanges with firstChange handles both cases.
How does the modern signal input approach change this?

With signal inputs I do not need ngOnChanges at all. I declare policyId = input.required<string>() and use computed(() => this.policyId()) to derive state or effect(() => this.loadPolicy(this.policyId())) to react to changes. Angular tracks the dependency automatically.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you restrict a service so it has a single instance per component (component-level providers)?

answer.oneLiner: Add the service to the component's providers array in @Component. Angular's hierarchical injector creates a fresh instance for each component instance and destroys it when the component is destroyed.
answer.detail: Angular's injector hierarchy runs from the platform injector at the top, down to root, environment injectors for lazy routes, and finally component injectors. A service registered with providedIn: 'root' is shared globally as a singleton. A service in a component's providers array is created fresh for each instance of that component and scoped to that component's subtree. Any child component that injects the service will receive the same instance created for the parent. When the component is destroyed, ngOnDestroy on the service fires and the instance is discarded. This is perfect for per-component state isolation - for example, a PaginationService per data grid instance.
keyPoints: Component providers create one service instance per component instance - destroyed with the component
Child components in the same subtree share the component-level instance
ngOnDestroy on the service is called when the component is destroyed
Compare: providedIn root = one shared singleton; viewProviders = only visible to own view, not ng-content

code.note: Two DataGrid components on the same page each get their own PaginationService with independent page state.

answer.analogy: A component-level service is like a coffee machine in each meeting room, not one shared machine for the whole floor.
redFlags: Thinking providedIn: 'root' can be scoped per component - it is always an app-wide singleton
Not knowing the service instance is destroyed with the component - important for cleanup in services with subscriptions
