Confusing providers with viewProviders - viewProviders hides the service from ng-content projected children

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between providers and viewProviders in a component?
followUpsQA.a: providers makes the service visible to the component's own template and any content projected via ng-content. viewProviders makes it visible only to own view children - projected content from outside cannot see it.

followUpsQA.q: What happens to a component-scoped service when the component is destroyed?
followUpsQA.a: Angular calls ngOnDestroy on the service, giving it a chance to clean up subscriptions or resources. The instance is then discarded by the injector.

followUpsQA.q: Do child components in the same subtree share the parent's component-level service?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - if the child does not provide its own instance, the injector walks up the tree and finds the instance created for the parent component.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use a lazy route's environment injector instead of a component provider?
followUpsQA.a: When you want a service shared across all components within a lazy-loaded feature, but isolated from the rest of the app. The lazy route creates an environment injector that scopes the service to its entire subtree.

dialogue.text: How do you give a service a single instance per component rather than a global singleton?

Add it to the component's providers array in @Component. Angular's hierarchical injector creates a fresh instance for each instance of that component. If I have two DataGrid components on the same page, each gets its own independent PaginationService.
What happens to that instance when the component is destroyed?

Angular calls ngOnDestroy on the service, so I can clean up subscriptions or WebSocket connections. Then the instance is discarded. This is automatic cleanup that you do not get with a root singleton.
Can child components in the same subtree access that service?

Yes - if a child injects the service and does not provide its own, the injector walks up the tree and finds the instance created for the parent. So the toolbar and rows inside a DataGrid both get the same PaginationService.
What is the difference between providers and viewProviders here?

providers is visible to both the component's own view children and any content projected via ng-content. viewProviders is visible only to own view children - projected content from outside cannot see it. Useful when you do not want a service leaking into third-party slot content.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

questions.q: How do you preserve form data when the user navigates away and comes back?

answer.oneLiner: Lift the form state out of the component into a shared service so it survives the component being destroyed. Use a CanDeactivate guard to warn the user before discarding unsaved changes, and sessionStorage for persistence across full page reloads.
answer.detail: When a user navigates away from a route, Angular destroys the component and all its local state including FormGroup values. To preserve that state, move the form values into a singleton service before the component is destroyed (or continuously via valueChanges) and rehydrate the form in ngOnInit when the component remounts. A CanDeactivate guard intercepts navigation and shows a confirmation dialog if form.dirty is true. For persistence across browser refresh, serialize the form value to sessionStorage or localStorage and restore it on init. A reactive approach is to connect the form to a signal store and persist the store.
keyPoints: Store form values in a singleton service or signal store - they survive component destruction
Rehydrate the form from the service in ngOnInit when the component remounts
CanDeactivate guard warns the user before navigating away with unsaved changes
Use sessionStorage or localStorage to persist across full page reloads

code.note: Auto-saving via valueChanges means the draft is always current without the user needing to click Save.

answer.analogy: Keeping state in a service is like leaving your work on the desk - destroying the component just turns the light off, not removes the desk.
redFlags: Keeping all form state inside the component - it is always lost when the user navigates away and back
Not having a CanDeactivate guard on long forms - losing data silently on accidental navigation is a serious UX issue
Not knowing the difference between sessionStorage (survives navigation, cleared on tab close) and localStorage (persists indefinitely)

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between using a service and sessionStorage for form persistence?
followUpsQA.a: A service persists form state across navigation within the current app session but is cleared on a full page refresh. sessionStorage also survives navigation and persists through refresh but is cleared when the browser tab is closed. localStorage persists indefinitely until explicitly cleared.

followUpsQA.q: How do you auto-save form state without the user clicking a save button?
followUpsQA.a: Subscribe to form.valueChanges and call a debounced save to the service or storage, so every change is captured without requiring explicit user action.

followUpsQA.q: How does CanDeactivate receive the component instance?
followUpsQA.a: The router passes the current component instance as the first argument to the CanDeactivateFn, allowing you to check form.dirty or any other component state directly.

followUpsQA.q: When would you use a signal store instead of a plain service?
followUpsQA.a: When the form feeds into a larger feature state that multiple components need to read - a signal store gives you reactive reads and cleaner state updates without BehaviorSubject boilerplate.

dialogue.text: How would you preserve form data when a user navigates away and then comes back to the form?

Lift the form state out of the component into a singleton service. I subscribe to form.valueChanges and auto-save every change to the service. When the user navigates back and the component remounts, ngOnInit reads the saved draft and patches the form.
What about warning the user before they accidentally lose their edits?

A CanDeactivate guard. The functional guard receives the component instance, checks form.dirty, and shows a confirmation dialog if there are unsaved changes. If the user confirms, navigation proceeds; otherwise it is cancelled.
What if the user refreshes the page? Service state is lost.

For a long form like a loan application, I serialize to sessionStorage on every valueChanges emission. On init I check sessionStorage first before falling back to an empty form. That way a refresh or crash does not lose progress.
What is the trade-off between sessionStorage and localStorage here?

sessionStorage is cleared when the tab closes, which is usually right for draft forms - you do not want a half-complete application from six months ago auto-populating. localStorage persists indefinitely, which suits settings or preferences rather than in-progress form data.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
